coredata.dc:title: Adsorption and gas-sensing investigation of oil dissolved gases onto nitrogen and sulfur doped graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Inah, Bassey E.
Eze, John F.
Louis, Hitler
Edet, Henry O.
Gber, Terkumbur E.
Eno, Ededet A.
Etim, Alice N.
Adeyinka, Adedapo S.
coredata.dc:description: Burning hydrocarbons as fuel, which produces carbon dioxide and water, is a major contributor to anthropogenic global warming. Hydrocarbons are introduced into the environment through their extensive use as fuels and chemicals as well as through leaks or accidental spills during exploration, production, refining, or transport of fossil fuels. Herein, theoretical calculations based on density functional theory (DFT) was applied to investigate the adsorption behavior of C2H4, CH4 and H2 on graphene quantum dot surfaces doped with Nitrogen (N) and sulfur (S) (GQD_N, GQD_S). Theoretical calculations in this study were obtained with the dispersion correction in consideration so as to predict intermolecular interactions alongside B3LYP-D3(BJ)/6-311+G (d, p). The sites that were doped with N and S atom were found to be more stable and suitable for gas adsorption. The adsorption energy was computed to establish the surface abilities of the adsorptions under investigation. Gas adsorptions on surfaces showed similar high negative values. We may deduce from the computed adsorption energies that GQD_N and GQD_S have strong adsorptions and considered adsorptions are thermodynamically favored. The ellipticity parameter calculated using the quantum theory of atoms in molecules (QTAIM), as well as the stabilization energies obtained from natural bond orbitals (NBO), confirmed the stability of surfaces upon gas adsorptions. QTAIM also confirmed remarkable intermolecular interactions. This result also agrees with that for non-covalent interactions, which predicted weak intermolecular interactions between surface and gas molecules. GQD_N and GQD_S are good adsorbents that can adsorb C2H4, CH4, and H2 gases, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Oil dissolved gases
Adsorption
Quantum dot
DFT

Quantum theory of atom in molecules (QTAIM)
Adsorption energy, charge transfer and thermodynamic properties
Ethics approval and consent to participate

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Pictorial representation of the optimized surfaces and gases C2H4, CH4 and H2.

a: Pictorial view of Absorption GQD_N_C2H4, CH4, HOMO, LUMO and the energy gap of Nitrogen graphene doped atom. b: Pictorial view of Absorption GQD_N_H2, GQD_S_C2H4, HOMO, LUMO and the energy gap of GQD_N c: Pictorial view of Absorption GQD_S_CH4, H2, HOMO, LUMO and the energy gap of GQD_S.
Plot of Density of state for studied adsorptions.
Molecular electrostatic potential for studied adsorptions.
RDG and VMD plot for studied adsorptions.
Calculated values for HOMO, LUMO and Energy gap for isolated and studied adsorptions.
Donor, acceptor and the perturbation energies of the absorption molecules calculated at B3LYP-D3(BJ)/ 6-311+G (d, p).
Selected topological parameters for studied adsorptions.
Calculated adsorption energies and distance of adsorption using B3LYP-D3(BJ)/6-311+G (d,p).

head.ce:title: Adsorption and gas-sensing investigation of oil dissolved gases onto nitrogen and sulfur doped graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Computational and Bio-Simulation Research Group, University of Calabar, Calabar, Nigeria
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aComputational and Bio-Simulation Research Group, University of Calabar, Calabar, Nigeria

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Physical Sciences, University of Cross River, Nigeria
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Chemistry, Faculty of Physical Sciences, University of Cross River, Nigeria

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical sciences, Research Centre for Synthesis and Catalysis, University of Johannesburg, South Africa

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Centre for Synthesis and Catalysis

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cDepartment of Chemical sciences, Research Centre for Synthesis and Catalysis, University of Johannesburg, South Africa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Burning hydrocarbons as fuel, which produces carbon dioxide and water, is a major contributor to anthropogenic global warming. Hydrocarbons are introduced into the environment through their extensive use as fuels and chemicals as well as through leaks or accidental spills during exploration, production, refining, or transport of fossil fuels. Herein, theoretical calculations based on density functional theory (DFT) was applied to investigate the adsorption behavior of C2H4, CH4 and H2 on graphene quantum dot surfaces doped with Nitrogen (N) and sulfur (S) (GQD_N, GQD_S). Theoretical calculations in this study were obtained with the dispersion correction in consideration so as to predict intermolecular interactions alongside B3LYP-D3(BJ)/6-311+G (d, p). The sites that were doped with N and S atom were found to be more stable and suitable for gas adsorption. The adsorption energy was computed to establish the surface abilities of the adsorptions under investigation. Gas adsorptions on surfaces showed similar high negative values. We may deduce from the computed adsorption energies that GQD_N and GQD_S have strong adsorptions and considered adsorptions are thermodynamically favored. The ellipticity parameter calculated using the quantum theory of atoms in molecules (QTAIM), as well as the stabilization energies obtained from natural bond orbitals (NBO), confirmed the stability of surfaces upon gas adsorptions. QTAIM also confirmed remarkable intermolecular interactions. This result also agrees with that for non-covalent interactions, which predicted weak intermolecular interactions between surface and gas molecules. GQD_N and GQD_S are good adsorbents that can adsorb C2H4, CH4, and H2 gases, respectively.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrocarbon refers to both petroleum and natural gas, which are two naturally occurring phases [1]. The burning of fossil fuels, including fuel production and combustion, accounts for the majority of anthropogenic greenhouse gas emissions. The emissions of vegetation are natural sources of hydrocarbons such ethylene, isoprene, and monoterpenes [2]. Burning hydrocarbons as fuel, which produces carbon dioxide and water, is a major contributor to anthropogenic global warming [3]. Hydrocarbons are introduced into the environment through their extensive use as fuels and chemicals as well as through leaks or accidental spills during exploration, production, refining, or transport of fossil fuels [4,5]. Insulation flaws will cause the decomposition of oil into various gases, including hydrogen and hydrocarbon gases like CH4 and C2H4 that dissolved in oil, in addition to the overheating and partial discharge in transformers [6]. Dissolved gas analysis (DGA) has proven a viable and practical approach of diagnosing the types and degree of insulation problems to assure transformer operational stability [7]. Conversely, Graphene has been shown in experiments to be highly sensitive to certain gas molecules such as NH3, CO, and NO2. According to some experimental and theoretical studies, graphene has a planar structure and has a very weak interaction with gas molecules [8].

Previous studies have shown that graphene modified with certain dopants has great gas sensing capabilities, and that it improves the selectivity and sensitivity to certain gases in particular [9]. Transition metal doped graphene offers the best gas-sensing performance of all the surface modification techniques. Within its 2-dimension structure, graphene as sp2 linked carbons possesses extraordinary features like a high surface-to-volume fraction and excellent transport capabilities [10]. It is known that graphene-like boron carbonitrides (Bx, Cy, & Nz) with huge surface areas ranging from 1500 to 2000 m2/g demonstrate strong CO2 absorption. Although numerous types of oxygen-containing functional groups, such as carboxyl, epoxy, carbonyl, and others, can be added to the surface of graphene to produce graphene oxide [11]. Furthermore, the presence of oxygen-containing groups not only provides chemical stability to graphene oxide, but also provides an active location for surface modification and a large specific surface area. As a result, graphene oxide has been reported to be a good sensor material [12]. Singh et al. [13] developed a NO2 gas sensor based on an rGO-coated clad etched fibre Bragg grating (EFBG). Rad, reported enhance adsorption effect of N-doped graphene [10]. The electrospinning approach was used to detect NH3 gas in Co3O4 nanocrystals, With the NH3 gas, and the sensor has the morphology of nanofibers and has a good sensitivity this was carried out by Feng et al. [14]. Kheirabadi and Vakili, studied the adsorption of C2H4 and CO2 on Se-G and Te-G surface. Also, Gui et al., also studied the adsorption of C2H2, CH4 on Mn-doped graphene and concluded that the adsorption energy of the gases on Mn-graphene has largely increased except CH4 due to the electronic hybridization [15,16]. Impeng et al., demonstrated that a MnN4 moiety graphene supercell may be used as a magnetic gas sensor for CO gas detection, with a magnetic moment of 1.04 µB generated upon adsorption [17]. Abbasi and Sardroodi, research and found that N-doped TiO2/WSe2 nanocomposite is more energetically favorable than the adsorption of O3 on the pristine one, representing that the N-doped nanocomposites are more sensitive than the undoped ones [18]. Similarly, Abbasi and Sadroodi, showed the capabilities of N-doped TiO2/MoS2 nanocomposites are greater than those of undoped nanocomposites, and the interaction of NOx molecules with N-doped nanocomposites is more energy-efficient than that of undoped nanocomposites. Cui et al. demonstrated that the Pd-HfSe2 monolayer has superior adsorption performance on NO2 molecules when compared to SO2 molecules, with higher adsorption energy and charge-transfer, resulting in a more sensitive electrical response for NO2 detection [19]. Abbasi and Sardroodi, highlighted that boron doping is energetically favorable than that of the pristine one for the adsorption of O3 and SO2 ga molecule [20]. This has an impact on our knowledge on the effect of dopants and improving the adsorption properties of nanomaterials.

Because of their high attenuation coefficient, quantum dots are highly interesting for optical applications [21]. The dopant effect in improving the sensitivity and selectivity of nanomaterials to gas molecules has been used to modify quantum dots for many applications, including gas adsorptions [22]. Quantum dots were chosen for our investigation because of their great properties and prior reports, and they were additionally doped with heteroatoms Nitrogen (N) and Sulfur (S) for adsorption of hydrocarbons such as C2H4, CH4, and H2, which have been hazardous in the environment. Adsorbing these hydrocarbons is crucial due to the damage they cause. Herein, the adsorption of hydrocarbons on graphene quantum dots doped surface (GQD_N and GQD_S) was ascertain using the frontier molecular orbital to confirm the reactivity and stability of surface in the isolated and adsorbed state. Theoretically, NCI analysis provides a link based on the electron density and its derivatives that enable identification of noncovalent interactions which is employed in interacting systems [23,24]. Non-covalent interactions (NCI) and reduced density gradient (RDG) were used to determine weak intermolecular interactions between the surface and the gas molecule, this analysis clearly describes the nature of interaction between the gas molecules and the adsorbing surface. Topology analysis was also done to determine the nature of adsorptions using the quantum theory of atom in molecules. Calculations for adsorption energies and thermodynamic properties were computed to confirm surface abilities for gas adsorptions and reaction path. Also, natural bond orbitals (NBO) were deployed to ascertain charge transfer, charge delocalization and stabilization of surface and adsorptions. Molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) analysis is also performed to see the change in electronic behavior while the distribution of HOMO, LUMO was plotted using the density of state (DOS).

The surface of a graphene quantum dot with a high surface to volume ratio has been doped with heteroatoms (N and S), which increases the surface's adsorption properties. All theoretical calculations in this study were based on density functional theory (DFT), Geometry optimization for isolated and adsorbed surfaces were carried out on Gaussian 16 software [25], using the Becke 3-parameter Lee–Yang–Parr (B3LYP) functional. The B3LYP density functional has been known to be suitable towards nanostructure studies, and was done with empirical dispersion (GD3BJ) at 6-311+G (d, p) level of theory. The DFT-GD3BJ is a widely known dispersion method which accounts for dispersion interactions and also helps in predicting weak intermolecular interactions. Frequency optimization at the same level of theory was computed to ensure isolated and adsorbed surface obtain potential energy minima and accurate calculations for zero-point energy corrections [26]. The embedded NBO 7.0 programme in Gaussian software was used for natural bond orbital analysis [27]. Checkpoint files of optimized adsorptions were used in multiwfn analyzer for QTAIM analysis and non-covalent interactions (NCI) to investigate the adsorption mechanism of surfaces. Though visual molecular dynamics (VMD) was used for visualizations aimed at NCI [28,29]. The intramolecular interaction existing between the surface and the gas molecule was also explained from the analysis of NCI [30,31]. Also, the distribution of HOMO and LUMO was achieved with the density of state plot also using multiwfn [32]. The map showing sites possible for electrophilic and nucleophilic attack as well as studying gas adsorption mechanism was done using molecular electrostatic potential (MESP) [31]. Thermodynamic properties and adsorption energies were calculated with the utilization of the equations

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stable geometries of doped graphene quantum dots

ce:section.ce:para: Frontier molecular orbital provides information for the interaction between the highest occupied molecular orbitals and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital's (HOMO, LUMO) [38]. Understanding FMOs is important for understanding chemical interactions, as well as developing new materials [39]. The interaction between the HOMO- LUMO is justified by the filled and unfilled orbital. From the theory of frontier molecular orbital, the HOMO acts as an electron donor while the LUMO acts as an electron acceptor [40]. Also, the reactivity, stability and conductivity of a molecule is determined by the value for energy gap which is the difference between the HOMO- LUMO energies. Also, the energy gap is also used to measure the stability, reactivity, sensitivity selectivity and other important characteristics of a molecule or adsorbed material [41,42]. This is necessary so as to confirm the adsorption properties of graphene quantum dot doped surfaces. Calculated values for energy gap and quantum chemical descriptors are visible in Table 1 . This was achieved using the B3LYP/6-311+G (GD3BJ) (d, p) level of theory. High energy gap describes molecule stability while low energy gap describes molecule reactivity. As elucidated in Table 1, the surface GQD_S was observed to have a lower value for energy gap (0.396 eV) which makes it reactive more than GQD_N with energy gap (2.556 eV), this could be due to the high electronegative nature of nitrogen [43]. In contrary the observed energy gap for GQD_N confirms it to be stable this could arise due to the nature of doped atom nitrogen. This implies that GQD_S is more reactive while GQD_N is more stable. Following the adsorption of gases; C2H4, CH4, and H2 on GQD_S and GQD_N. The energy gap found after adsorption on the GQD_S surface ranges from 1.701 to 1.708 eV respectively, with GQD_S_C2H4 (1.701 eV), GQD_S_CH4 (1.707 eV), and GQD_S_H2 (1.708 eV). Increased energy gap for GQD_S is due to sulfur ability to bond with nearly all other elements which is in line with report by Xu et al. [44]. For adsorptions on GQD_N surface, energy gap values ranged between 2.016 eV and 2.557 eV respectively. GQD_N C2H4 (2.563 eV), GQD_N_CH4 (2.557 eV), and GQD_N_H2 (2.557 eV) respectively. Obviously, similarity in energy gap for the isolated and adsorbed state of GQD_N is due to covalent bond between nitrogen and hydrogen atoms observed in studied gases. From the FMO results, it was indicated that surfaces under investigation become more conductive and stable when they adsorb gases. Therefore, they are good candidate for gas adsorption. Fig. 2a-2c , shows that the HOMO and LUMO of the graphene quantum dot doped surfaces is mostly localized on the C-C bonds.

Density of state (DOS) analysis was performed to evaluate and explain the differences in characteristics between doped graphene quantum dot surface GQD_N and GQD_S, so as to analyze the gas sensing mechanism and adsorption properties of selected gas molecules: C2H4, CH4 and H2 on considered surfaces. As shown in Fig. 3 , DOS was plotted in the range of −2.0 a.u. to 25.0 a. u and −2.0 a. u to 27.0 a. u for both GQD_N and GQD_S. The DOS plots reveal that the electronic state near the Fermi level decreases due to the S dopant atom. It is important also to point out that due to the large scale of the non – overlapping area; the plots observed in Fig. 3 are quite different. The length of the C-S bond proves the strong chemical interaction between C atoms and the Sulphur doped on graphene. The relatively wide DOS near Fermi level implies that the highest occupied levels have many electrons that can seldom transit to the conducting band [45], the title surfaces' conductivity was found to be good, implying that the doped surfaces are good sensing materials. We employed multiwfn software for characterization of the DOS of the investigated surfaces [32]. From the DOS plot, TDOS represent total density of states, PDOS is the partial density of state which is contributed from the individual orbitals present in the studied adsorptions meanwhile, OPDOS is the overlap partial density of state curve. The OPDOS observed here shows antibonding characteristics, which could better be explained on the basis of the unfavorable overlapping of orbitals [42]. It is clear that occupied frontier MOs are solely contributed by carbon. The axis at left side corresponds to TDOS and PDOS, while the one at right side corresponds to OPDOS. The red, blue, pink and green discrete lines represent PDOS of Carbon, Hydrogen Nitrogen and OPDOS, respectively for GQD_N Meanwhile for GQD_S blue represent the S discrete line. It can be seen that in most valence MOs, the carbon atoms have reasonable amount of contribution.

To provide detailed information about the most probable site for electrophilic and nucleophilic attacks on the studied absorptions, molecular electrostatic potential mapping which is an essential tool that provide understanding of the electron density was carried out [46], using the B3LYP/6-311+G (d, P) (GD3BJ) level of theory and the result obtained is presented in Fig. 4 . This density of electrons is used to indicate the charge distribution within or around a molecule produced by the nuclei and electrons as a critical concept in wavefunction analysis. MESP also gives detailed explanation about the intermolecular interaction between the surface and gas molecule [47]. As displayed in Fig. 4, the electron density on the atomic sites is presented with different colors. Red being the more negative, and white being more positive. The electron density varies in the order red > green > blue > pink > white. From the map it is evident that the electron rich regions are located on the Nitrogen and Oxygen atoms, while the major electron deficient regions are located over the hydrogen atoms. The negative regions are related to electrophilic reactivity and the positive regions to nucleophilic reactivity. MESP provides a visual representation of the chemically active sites and comparative reactivity of the atoms. Literature review shows that the more chemically active sites indicate the more absorption sites [48,49].

The natural bond orbital analysis was used in this study to provides a detailed information about inter and intramolecular interactions for the isolated surfaces and studied adsorptions [50]. It is a computational tool which utilizes the Schrodinger equation to explain the chemical bonding concept in molecules [51]. The interactions between almost filled donor Lewis's structure and the empty acceptor non-Lewis structure is studied from this analysis. The “Lewis”-type referred to as the (donor) NBOs are thereby complemented by the “non-Lewis”-type which are (acceptor) NBOs that are formally empty in an idealized Lewis structure picture. Weak occupancies of the valence anti-bonds signal irreducible departures from an idealized localized Lewis structure picture, i.e., true “delocalization effects.” Basically, natural bond orbital analysis is employed in studying the donor and acceptor orbitals interactions in molecules and provide more details in the understanding of inter and intra-molecular interactions and bonding [52]. In this analysis, the second order perturbation Fock matrix was calculated to ascertain the donor (Lewis Valance Orbital) i, and acceptor (non -Lewis) Valance orbital) j, interactions in the NBO basis [53]. The E(2) energy (Stabilization energy) associated with the delocalization of electron between the filled (Lewis) and unfilled (non-Lewis) are estimated herein using Eq. (4):
(5)
E
( 2 )
=
Δ
E
i , j
= −
q i
F
2
(
i , j
)
ε
i
−
ε
j

From equation one (1), Fij is the off-diagonal element on the Fock matricx, E(j) - E(i) are the diagonal elements and E (2) represent the second order stabilization energy of the studied systems respectively [49]. The second order perturbation energy analysis in this study was carried out in two phases, before interaction of the graphene with the doped atoms and the stabilization energy after the interaction of the N and S dope atoms. In each case the second order energy was used as basis for determining the strength of the interaction. Much information about natural bond orbital is presented in Table 2 , from the table it can be seen that five major transitions exist between the donor and the acceptor orbitals which include π* → σ*, π* → π*, π* → σ, σ → σ*, σ → π* out of which the strongest interaction was observed from π* → σ* transitions. Other transitions such as both the extra – valence Rydberg (RY) type and the Core (Cr) were also observed though, but were not much concentrated on since they are loosely bonded orbitals and do not have much information on the absorption properties of the studied doped atoms. It has been established that the higher the perturbation energy, the stronger or intense the interaction at that region and the stronger the absorption mechanism of the interacting species. So we observed here that before interaction, Nitrogen doped graphene have higher perturbation energies compared to S doped graphene, this could be because of the higher electronegativity and small size of Nitrogen over the Sulphur. The strongest interaction before interaction was observed from σC1 – C13
→ σ*C14 – C45 with the observed perturbation energy value of 99,665.27 kcal/mol. Several energies as displayed in Table 2 were observed which result into the basis studying the inter charge transfer from the donor to the acceptor orbital.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum theory of atom in molecules (QTAIM)

ce:section.ce:para:
Non-covalent interaction (NCI) was applied to study the intermolecular and intramolecular interactions between or within molecules through hydrogen, electrostatic, steric hindrance and van der Waals forces [30]. Likewise, they are also involved in a host of biological processes where large molecules bind specifically. These interactions are significant for drug design, synthesis of organic molecules, design of materials for adsorption as well as others [50]. Interpretation of electron density for various adsorptions between surface and gases was achieved using VMD software [25]. Reduced density gradient (RDG) is a function along with λ2(r)p(r) in non-covalent interactions used to visualize regions of weak interactions [53], regions of weak interactions is plotted in specific areas using RDG and sign λ2(r)p(r) [30]. Values in atomic unit on the Y-axis gives information regarding RDG relative proportion whereas values on the X-axis relates to sign of interactions [54]. Strong interactions are indicated at points were sign λ2(r)ρ (r) < 0, while weak van der Waals interactions are found in the sign λ2(r)ρ(r) ≈ 0 region. At points where sign λ2(r)ρ(r) > 0 interactions are said to be repulsive Doust et al. [60]. Low reduced density gradient and low electron density regions is examined to determine non-covalent interactions between the adsorbent and adsorbate. It can be shown that spots appear in the sign λ2(r)p(r) ≈ 0 region upon adsorption of studied gases. This was also reported by Doust and Abdullah, for gas adsorptions on BBNT surface [58]. when using an isosurface as a reference (e.g., RDG = 0.5). RDG is defined as follows;
(6)
R D G =
1
2
( 3
π
2
)
1 3
|
Δ
ρ
¯
( r )
|
ρ ( r )
¯
4 3

As seen in Fig. 4. for colored surface map obtained using multiwfn program [28], upon adsorptions of C2H4, CH4 and H2 on GQD_N surface, weak van der waals interactions with steric hindrances were observed as λ2(r)p(r) ≈ 0, In stated adsorptions, regions of vdW contacts occurred between hydrogen atoms in gases and doped nitrogen atoms on graphene, forming a covalent connection when the N-H bond engages, which plays a crucial role in the adsorption process and indicates strong adsorption. For GQD_S surface, vdW interactions with steric effect were observed upon adsorption of studied gases. Electropositive effect of hydrogen atom from studied gases upon interaction with sulfur atom from surface resulted in weak intermolecular interactions as λ2(r)p(r) ≈ 0. In reference to previous research by Doust and Abdullah, weak interactions observed for the adsorption of cysteine molecule onto BNNT and BNAlNT which implied strong adsorptions [60]. Moreso, steric effect is evident in ring of surface for studied adsorptions as it implies stability of graphene surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption energy, charge transfer and thermodynamic properties
ce:section.ce:para: Several configurations of C2H4, CH4 and H2 gases on graphene quantum dot doped with nitrogen and sulfur atoms (GQD_N, GQD_S) surface were investigated to find the most stable site for adsorptions with the largest adsorption energy [55,56]. Upon adsorption of gases difference in geometric properties was observed. Fig. 21a-2c and Table 4 shows stable site for adsorption and calculated adsorption energy. Large negative value for adsorption energy confirms better adsorption and favored thermodynamic properties. Adsorptions of studied gases on surface is said to be exothermic and spontaneous with observed negative values for adsorption. In this work, C2H4 was better adsorbed in GQD_S with adsorption energy of −0.77 eV compared to −0.76 eV for GQD_N. Also, adsorption of CH4 and H2 on surface GQD_S and GQD_N exhibited a deviation of −0.2. Similarly, a deviation of −0.2 was observed for the adsorption of H2 on GQD_N with −1.03 while GQD_S with −1.05. We can emphasize that obtained Eads is similar to that reported by Chen et al., for the adsorption of C2H4, CH4 and H2 on metal oxides, and that by Gui et al., for the adsorption of C2H4, CH4 and H2 on modified GeSe monolayer [61,62]. The dispersion effect predicts adsorption energies accurately. Nevertheless, in line with previous research by Makino et al., that researched experimentally and found the possibility of C2H4 on stepped cupper surface [63], therein our observations are in tandem. Conversely, the examined surfaces' complying adsorbing properties could be related to the covalent nature of the link between adsorbate and adsorbent, as doped elements such as nitrogen and sulfur interact well with hydrogen atoms from gases. This result is similar to that for frontier molecular orbital and non-covalent interactions. We can infer that due to similar values for adsorption energy for GQD_S and GQD_N, surfaces are suitable for adsorbing studied gases. Also, the Charge transfer is significant in adsorption research because it influences the intensity of the connection between adsorbate and adsorbent. When electrons are transported from the adsorbate to the adsorbent, the electrostatic attraction that results between the two species might strengthen the adsorption link. When electrons are transported from the adsorbent to the adsorbate, the electrostatic repulsion that results can weaken the adsorption strength. The results of the charge transfer analysis (Qt) in this study are presented in Table 1, from which all the calculated values were observed to be native. Literature review shows that negative charge transfer can have a significant impact on the chemical properties and reactivity of molecules, as it can lead to the formation of new chemical bonds and the creation of charged species.

In this study, the adsorption and gas-sensing properties of graphene quantum dot doped with nitrogen and sulfur (GQD_N, GQD_S) upon three oil dissolved gases: C2H4 CH4 and H2 were investigated based on DFT calculations. Various adsorption structures of gas molecules were built to explore the most stable structures. Then, adsorption energy, DOS, and molecular orbital theory, QTAIM, NCI and MESP were computed and used to analyze the adsorption behavior and gas-sensing mechanism. The following conclusions can be drawn:
1
Two adsorptions’ sites were observed. The sites that were doped with N and S and the site at the centre of a CC bond. The doped atom sites were found to be more stable and suited for gas adsorption.
2
Calculated values for energy gap indicates that surfaces are conducive to gas adsorption. Also, Calculated adsorption energy confirms surface abilities for gas adsorptions. Gas adsorptions on surfaces showed similar high negative values. We may deduce from the computed adsorption energies that GQD_N and GQD_S have strong adsorptions and that adsorptions are thermodynamically favored.
3
non-covalent interactions (NCI) predicted weak intermolecular interactions between surface and gas molecules. However, adsorptions were stable due to value of ellipticity city ε < 1. A remarkable intermolecular interaction was also observed from quantum theory of atom in molecules (QTAIM).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
ce:section.ce:para: Not applicable.
Not applicable.
This work was not funded by any agency.

Hitler Louis: Project conceptualization, design, supervision, and administration. Inah Bassey: Writing, analysis, and manuscript draft. John F. Eze and Henry O. Edet: Review, writing, analysis, and manuscript draft. Terkumbur E. Gber: Analysis and writing. Alice Ndem Etim, and Ededet A. Eno: Analysis, writing, and editing. Adedapo S. Adeyinka: Resources, review, and editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the centre for high performance computing (CHPC), South Africa for providing computational resources for this research project.
